title: Sewn headbands on a rolled-paper core
subtitle: Working coloured silks over a cord to make the traditional bookbinding head and tail decoration
excerpt: A sewn headband is worked over a cord core at the head and tail of the spine. The needle alternates between the cord and the outermost signature, building a chain of diagonal silk stitches that are both structural and decorative. It takes about 20 minutes per headband once the rhythm is established.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), public domain, chapter on headbands. Cockerell illustrates both single-colour and two-colour headband patterns; the structure he describes is the basis of this tutorial.

glossaryTerms.definition: A decorative and lightly structural cord sewn at the head and tail of a text block. Historically sewn onto the spine itself; since the nineteenth century, usually machine-made and glued on. A sewn headband is the hand-made original.
The cord or rolled paper over which headband silk is sewn. The core gives the headband its rounded profile and serves as the carrier for the stitches.

content.text: sold for case-binding are woven on a machine and glued on as a strip. The sewn original is worked stitch by stitch over a

using a bookbinding needle and coloured silk thread. The stitches simultaneously anchor the spine's head or tail signature and wrap the core in a spiral pattern. Two colours can be alternated to produce a striped headband.

items.name: Headband core: a tightly rolled strip of text paper or a 2 mm linen cord
items.description: Roll a 10 mm strip of cartridge paper tightly around itself to produce a 2-3 mm diameter core; tie it with a few wraps of thread to secure. Alternatively, use a 2 mm linen cord. The core runs the full width of the spine.

items.name: Silk thread or stranded cotton in one or two colours
items.description: Period headbands used silk. Stranded cotton embroidery thread (all six strands) is a practical substitute. Colours are a personal choice; contrasting colours show the pattern most clearly.

items.name: Headband form / bookbinding needle (slug: headband-form, bookbinding-needle)
items.description: The needle must be blunt enough not to split the paper but fine enough to pass between stitches.

content.text: The headband is worked after the text block is sewn and spine-glued but before casing in. Hold the text block upright with the head facing up. Tie the thread to one end of the core and anchor the core against the head of the text block spine, running from one shoulder to the other. Use a short length of thread to lash the core ends to the shoulder paper at each side so the core stays in position during sewing.

Starting at the right-hand shoulder, pass the needle through the outermost signature at the head, just above the topmost sewing station: not through the sewing hole itself but slightly above it. Bring the thread over the core and loop it under the thread coming up from the signature. Pull the loop snug against the core. This is one stitch: an interlocking loop that grips both the core and the signature.

Move one stitch-width to the left and repeat: through the signature, over the core, under the loop. Each stitch should advance approximately 2 mm along the spine. The stitches build up into a diagonal pattern across the core face. For a two-colour headband, switch threads every two stitches; the colours spiral around the core.

When the left shoulder is reached, pass the thread under the last two stitches on the back of the core and trim, leaving 5 mm. The thread tucks under the stitches and does not need knotting. Check that the headband core projects no more than 2 mm above the spine on each side; trim the core ends if they project further. Work the tail headband in the same way with the book held head down.
